summary: Spolsky critiques the recurring media narrative that new programming tools will make software development as easy as snapping together Lego bricks. He traces this claim from a 1991 BusinessWeek cover story about object-oriented programming to a 2006 piece by Om Malik about new development platforms, showing how the same promise keeps resurfacing without ever materializing. He contrasts these optimistic predictions with Fred Brooks' 1987 argument in 'No Silver Bullet' that the inherent difficulty of software lies in conceptual design, not in the mechanics of coding. The post implies that journalists consistently misunderstand what makes programming hard.
key_points: Media coverage of programming tools repeatedly falls for vendor claims that new technology will make coding trivially easy, often using the Lego metaphor
The same 'snap-together programming' narrative appeared in 1991 with OOP and again in 2006 with web platforms, yet the fundamental difficulty of software persists
Fred Brooks identified in 1987 that the hard part of software is specification, design, and conceptual integrity — not the labor of writing code
Journalists conflate making syntax easier with making programming easier, missing that complexity is inherent to the problem domain
money_quotes: Frequently, the mainstream media, reporting on computer programming tools, gets the story horribly wrong.

What happens is that some kind of vendor of programming technologies has come up with some product they are claiming makes programming easier. The journalists don't really understand. What they hear is 'programming is going to be easier.'
Not only are there no silver bullets now in view, the very nature of software makes it unlikely that there will be any.

I believe the hard part of building software to be the specification, design, and testing of this conceptual construct, not the labor of representing it and testing the fidelity of the representation.
If this is true, building software will always be hard. There is inherently no silver bullet.
key_insight: The media perennially falls for the claim that new tools will make programming as easy as assembling Lego bricks, ignoring Brooks' insight that software's difficulty is inherent in the conceptual design, not in the mechanics of coding.
